ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for beef and radish soup; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef and radish soup is approximately 300 g, typically served as a side dish or light main at a Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: Beef and Radish Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef and Radish Soup (cr_id F06040001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef broth / lean beef soup data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on clear beef broth with low-carbohydrate vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef and radish soup. Carbohydrate content is very low at 0.4 g/100 g (1.2 g per 300 g serving). GI is estimated at approximately 32 based on analogous low-carbohydrate clear meat soups and daikon radish (GI ~15–35 range per University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (32 × 1.2) / 100 = 0.38, rounded to 0.4. The extremely low GL means GI classification has negligible practical impact on blood glucose.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 149 mg/100 g; low-sodium broth with potassium-rich daikon radish
hypertension.guidance: At 149 mg sodium per 100 g, this soup is well within DASH dietary guidelines; however, patients with hypertension should prepare it with homemade unsalted stock and avoid commercial broth, which can raise sodium to over 400 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 0.4 g/100 g; glycemic load per serving 0.4; high protein 4.4 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic load of 0.4 per standard serving, this soup has a negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose; the high protein content from lean beef further slows gastric emptying and supports glycaemic stability, making it a suitable choice for people managing type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (beef) content; low total fat 0.7 g/100 g; low saturated fat ~0.3 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Total fat is low at 0.7 g/100 g and saturated fat is estimated at approximately 0.3 g/100 g, which is within acceptable limits; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat consumption to moderate frequency (no more than 2–3 times per week) in line with AHA dietary guidelines, and should ensure the soup is prepared without added animal fats or high-sodium stock.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) is a source of arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis dietary management due to its arachidonic acid content; patients with RA should substitute beef with chicken, fish, or tofu to retain the nutritional benefits of the soup while eliminating the pro-inflammatory red meat component.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and Radish Soup (牛肉萝卜汤; Sup daging lembu dan lobak) is a light, clear Singaporean Chinese soup made by slow-simmering lean beef pieces with Chinese white radish (daikon), peppercorns, star anise, and spring onion. The result is a mildly savoury, slightly peppery broth with tender beef and soft, subtly sweet radish. The soup is low in calories (26 kcal/100 g), low in fat (0.7 g/100 g), and provides a meaningful amount of protein (4.4 g/100 g) from the lean beef. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.4 g/100 g, making the glycaemic load per standard 300 g bowl extremely low at approximately 0.4. Sodium is 149 mg/100 g, which is moderate and compatible with DASH dietary principles when prepared with homemade stock. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is commonly served as a side soup at Singaporean family meals, in hawker centres, and in traditional Chinese home cooking. Clinically, it is well-suited for individuals managing type 2 diabetes and hypertension due to its low carbohydrate and moderate sodium profile. It is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. The daikon radish contributes dietary fibre (0.4 g/100 g), vitamin C (~5 mg/100 g), and potassium (~180 mg/100 g), adding micronutrient value to the broth.

Is beef radish soup good for diabetics?

Is beef and radish soup low sodium?
Can I eat beef radish soup if I have high blood pressure?

Beef and radish soup for weight loss
Sup daging lembu dan lobak kalori
meal_context: side soup at Singaporean Chinese family meals

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06040001) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central lean beef soup data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for low-carbohydrate clear meat soups. Potassium and vitamin C values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central daikon radish and lean beef broth data and are clearly flagged.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for lean beef broth and lean beef (USDA SR Legacy item 13365, beef, chuck, lean, cooked, simmered) as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. 2. Potassium (~180 mg/100 g) and vitamin C (~5 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central daikon radish (FDC ID 169276) and lean beef broth component data; these are approximate and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. 3. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this traditional Singaporean beef and radish soup preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g (fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish; USDA FoodData Central does not carry a direct match), polyunsaturated_fat_g (same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g), trans_fat_g (not reported by HPB or USDA for this dish preparation), cholesterol_mg (not reported by HPB Foodo for this dish; USDA FoodData Central closest match does not include a composite soup entry), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (not reported by HPB; daikon and lean beef broth data in USDA FoodData Central do not yield a reliable composite figure), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (not reported by HPB; composite soup value not available in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (same basis as vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (same basis as vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg), vitamin_b6_mg (same basis as vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg), vitamin_b12_mcg (same basis as vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg), folate_mcg_dfe (not reported by HPB; composite value not available in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation), vitamin_d_mcg (not reported by HPB; not reliably available for this soup preparation in USDA FoodData Central), vitamin_e_mg (same basis as vitamin_d_mcg), phosphorus_mg (not reported by HPB Foodo for this dish; USDA FoodData Central does not carry a direct composite match), magnesium_mg (same basis as phosphorus_mg), zinc_mg (same basis as phosphorus_mg), selenium_mcg (same basis as phosphorus_mg), water_g (moisture content not separately reported by HPB Foodo for this dish and not available as a reliable composite in USDA FoodData Central). 4. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for beef and radish soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The practical impact is negligible given the very low carbohydrate content (0.4 g/100 g) and glycemic load (0.4 per 300 g serving). 5. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.3 from an 'Embedded GI Database'; this has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as required by Project Veritas v2 source policy.
